rationale: In the Experiment 2 method section, the paper explicitly cites Okada (2005) (with Okada and Hoch 2004) to justify measuring "purchase intention" via actual effort expenditure. This is more than a passing background list because the citation supports a concrete design choice and interpretation of the dependent variable. However, the paper does not build on Okada's hedonic-versus-utilitarian justification theory as its main contribution, so the role is applied rather than foundational to the full article.
relation_description: Ding and Zhang apply Okada (2005)'s idea that consumers can trade nonmonetary resources (time/effort) for goods to justify an effort-based acquisition task in Experiment 2. They use this logic to treat willingness to solve math problems as a purchase-intention measure.
